title: Use of potentially insecure workflow triggers in CI
body:
Three workflows in `.github/workflows` for this repo (`first_contrib_cert_generator.yml`, `labeler.yml`, and `machine-os-pr.yml`) use a `pull_request_target` workflow. [Zizmor flags this as dangerous](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#dangerous-triggers):

> These triggers are dangerous because they run in the context of the *target repository* rather than the *fork repository*, while also being typically triggerable by the latter. This can lead to attacker controlled code execution or unexpected action runs with context controlled by a malicious fork.

To be clear, I'm not aware of any specific security vulnerability with these workflows as written (hence why I'm making a public issue rather than disclosing it privately). However, it's still best practice to avoid the use of this workflow trigger when possible, and to audit the code especially carefully if the use of this trigger is unavoidable. (For context, see [this discussion](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27642#issuecomment-3602144692).)

So, can these workflows be adapted to use a different trigger? And if not, how do we ensure that their usage is secure?

comments.body: (`labeler.yml` is almost certainly fine since all it does is call [actions/labeler](https://github.com/actions/labeler), so this is really about the other two workflows.)

Unfortunately I'm not sure there's a way around using pull_request_target for the machine-os-pr, as we need to read the updated version number from the PR. I guess the best way to secure it is to make sure everything is properly escaped? But if anyone sees any way to use a more secure trigger, I'm all ears.

Yeah I am not sure either how to get rid of these.
I guess the main thing would be the first_contrib_cert_generator thing. I don't think that needs to be part of the PR. I suppose it could be run in other contexts as well, i.e. on merge (push) on main though then I guess it is the same issue where it would need to figure out the PR number somehow.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
